pattern: A word only qualifies when two independent frequency facts both hold at once, so tallying each list separately first and then intersecting the singleton keys is both simpler and faster than any pairwise comparison.
hints: Count how many times each word occurs in the first list, and separately how many times each word occurs in the second list.
A word only counts toward the answer if it occurs exactly once in the first list AND exactly once in the second list -- both conditions are required together, not either one alone.

Iterate the keys of one frequency map (the smaller one, for a small efficiency win) and check the other map's count for that same key, remembering that a word entirely missing from the other list simply has a count of zero there, not an error.

edge_inputs.description: a mix of singleton overlaps, a repeated word, and a word absent from the other list
two lists with no shared words at all
a word repeated three times in one list must not be counted even though it also appears in the other list
single-word lists that match exactly once each
single-word lists that do not match at all
identical lists where every word already appears more than once, so nothing qualifies
a word unique in the first list but appearing twice in the second list must not count
every word in both lists is unique and every word overlaps, so all qualify

stress.description: random lowercase word arrays sampled from a shared vocabulary pool so the overlap between the two lists is meaningful
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    pool_size = max(5, n // 3)
    pool = []
    for _ in range(pool_size):
        length = rng.randint(1, 30)
        word = ''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(length))
        pool.append(word)
    def make_list():
        length = rng.randint(1, n)
        return [rng.choice(pool) for _ in range(length)]
    return [make_list(), make_list()]
